title: Articles: der, die, das
summary: Every German noun has a gender: masculine (der), feminine (die), or neuter (das). The article changes based on the noun's case (nominative, accusative, dative, genitive). There are no foolproof rules — gender must usually be learned with the noun. The plural article is always 'die' in nominative/accusative.

examples.de: der Mann, die Frau, das Kind
examples.en: the man, the woman, the child
I see the man. (accusative — der → den)

examples.de: Ich gebe dem Mann ein Buch.
examples.en: I give the man a book. (dative — der → dem)

tips: Always learn nouns WITH their article: 'das Buch', not just 'Buch'.
Diminutives ending in -chen or -lein are always neuter (das Mädchen, das Brötchen).

title: Present Tense (Präsens)
summary: German present tense covers both 'I do' and 'I am doing'. Regular verbs follow predictable endings; many common verbs (sein, haben, gehen, kommen) are slightly irregular. Some strong verbs change their stem vowel in the du- and er/sie/es-forms.

examples.de: ich mache, du machst, er macht
examples.en: I do, you do, he does (regular)

examples.de: ich spreche, du sprichst, er spricht
examples.en: I speak, you speak, he speaks (e → i stem change)

examples.de: ich fahre, du fährst, er fährt
examples.en: I drive, you drive, he drives (a → ä stem change)

tips: If a verb stem ends in -t or -d, add an extra -e before -st/-t (du arbeitest).
Strong verbs only change stem in 2nd/3rd person singular.

title: sein and haben
summary: 'sein' (to be) and 'haben' (to have) are the two most important verbs in German. Both are irregular and used constantly — both as full verbs and as auxiliaries to form the Perfekt past tense.

examples.de: ich bin, du bist, er ist, wir sind, ihr seid, sie sind
examples.en: I am, you are, he is, we are, you (pl) are, they are

examples.de: ich habe, du hast, er hat, wir haben, ihr habt, sie haben
examples.en: I have, you have, he has, we have, you (pl) have, they have

examples.de: Ich habe gegessen. / Ich bin gegangen.
examples.en: I have eaten. / I have gone. (Perfekt auxiliaries)

tips: Memorize the conjugations cold — you'll use them in every conversation.
Movement verbs (gehen, fahren, kommen) take 'sein' in Perfekt; most others take 'haben'.
'sein' and 'haben' also have very common Präteritum forms: war, hatte.

title: Pronouns: ich, du, Sie, er, sie, es
summary: German distinguishes between informal (du) and formal (Sie) 'you', and has three genders for 'he/she/it' (er, sie, es) that match the noun's grammatical gender — not biological sex. Pronouns also change form across cases.

examples.de: Wo ist der Stuhl? — Er steht dort.
examples.en: Where is the chair? — It stands there. (der Stuhl → er, not 'es')
Do you know her? (informal 'du', 'sie' = her in accusative)
Can you (formal) help me? ('Sie' is always capitalized)

tips: Use 'Sie' with strangers, older people, and in professional settings; 'du' with friends, family, kids.
Capitalized 'Sie' = formal you (sing. + pl.); lowercase 'sie' = she or they.
The pronoun for a German noun matches the noun's gender, not English logic.

title: Word Order: Verb-Second Rule
summary: In main clauses, the conjugated verb is ALWAYS in second position — but what comes first is flexible. You can start with the subject, a time expression, an object, etc., and the verb still stays in slot two. This gives German much of its rhythm.

examples.en: I'm going to the cinema tomorrow. (Subject first)
Tomorrow I'm going to the cinema. (Time first — verb still second!)
I'm going to the cinema tomorrow. (Object first — emphasis)

tips: Count 'positions', not 'words': 'Letzten Sommer' counts as one position.
Yes/no questions break the rule — verb goes first: 'Gehst du ins Kino?'
In subordinate clauses (after weil, dass, wenn...), the verb goes to the END instead.
